title: Atomic structure — what everything is made of
examTip: AQA most commonly tests: calculating neutron number from mass number and atomic number; identifying isotopes; and explaining why relative atomic mass is not a whole number.
introTips: You'll learn the three subatomic particles, where they live, and what charge and mass each one has — this comes up on virtually every chemistry paper.
You'll also learn how to use the two numbers on the periodic table (atomic number and mass number) to work out exactly what's inside any atom.
The history of the atomic model is a favourite six-mark question — you need to know Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr, and what experiment or idea changed each one.
intro: Here's a strange thought: most of an atom is empty space. If you scaled the nucleus of a hydrogen atom up to the size of a marble, the electron would be orbiting about a kilometre away. Everything you can see and touch — your phone, this screen, you — is almost entirely nothing. Understanding atoms is the foundation of all chemistry, and AQA tests it on every single paper. Get this solid and everything else builds on it.

keyPoints.heading: The three subatomic particles
keyPoints.content: Every atom is built from three types of particle. Protons have a relative mass of 1 and a charge of +1 — they live in the nucleus at the centre. Neutrons also have a relative mass of 1 but no charge at all (charge = 0) — they also live in the nucleus. Electrons are almost massless (so tiny we treat their mass as 0) and carry a charge of −1 — they orbit the nucleus in shells (also called energy levels). In a neutral atom, the number of electrons always equals the number of protons, so the charges cancel out perfectly.
writeBullets: Proton: mass = 1, charge = +1, found in the nucleus
Neutron: mass = 1, charge = 0, found in the nucleus
Electron: mass ≈ 0, charge = −1, found in shells around the nucleus
Neutral atom: number of electrons = number of protons

keyTerms.def: Subatomic particle in the nucleus — relative mass 1, charge +1
Subatomic particle in the nucleus — relative mass 1, charge 0
Subatomic particle in shells around the nucleus — mass ≈ 0, charge −1
The tiny, dense, positively charged centre of an atom containing protons and neutrons

keyPoints.memTip: PEN: Protons (+1), Electrons (−1), Neutrons (0). Draw a small circle for the nucleus and label P and N inside it; draw dotted rings around it for electrons. The visual sticks much better than a list. Cover it and redraw from memory — three times does it.

keyPoints.heading: Atomic number and mass number
keyPoints.content: Every element on the periodic table has two key numbers. The atomic number (also called the proton number) is the smaller of the two — it tells you how many protons are in the nucleus. Because a neutral atom has equal protons and electrons, it also tells you the number of electrons. The mass number is the larger number — it's the total count of protons plus neutrons. So to find the number of neutrons, you just do: neutrons = mass number − atomic number. That subtraction comes up constantly in exams.
writeBullets: Atomic number = number of protons (= number of electrons in a neutral atom)
Mass number = protons + neutrons
Number of neutrons = mass number − atomic number
Example: sodium (Na) — atomic number 11, mass number 23 → 12 neutrons

keyTerms.def: The number of protons in the nucleus — defines which element it is
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus

keyPoints.memTip: Remember: the SMALLER number = atomic number (protons). The BIGGER number = mass number (protons + neutrons). Neutrons = Big minus Small. Practise with five elements from the periodic table right now — pick one you've never tried before each time.

keyPoints.heading: Isotopes — same element, different neutrons
keyPoints.content: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. Because the atomic number (protons) is unchanged, it's still the same element — it just has a different mass number. Carbon is the classic example: carbon-12 has 6 protons and 6 neutrons; carbon-14 has 6 protons and 8 neutrons. Both are carbon, both behave chemically the same way (because their electron arrangement is identical), but carbon-14 is heavier. Isotopes have the same chemical properties but different physical properties (like density and stability).
writeBullets: Isotopes = same element, same atomic number, different mass number
Same number of protons but different number of neutrons
Same chemical properties (same electron arrangement)
Example: carbon-12 (6p, 6n) and carbon-14 (6p, 8n)

keyTerms.def: Atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons

keyPoints.memTip: Isotopes: SAME protons, DIFFERENT neutrons. A useful way to remember: ISO sounds like 'identical' — identical atomic number. Use the carbon-12/carbon-14 pair as your anchor example: write it out until you can do it cold. 6 protons always, neutrons change (6 or 8).

keyPoints.heading: Relative atomic mass — why it's not a whole number
keyPoints.content: If you look at chlorine on the periodic table, its relative atomic mass (Ar) is 35.5 — not a whole number, even though each individual atom has a whole number of protons and neutrons. This is because relative atomic mass is the weighted average of the masses of all the naturally occurring isotopes of an element, taking into account how abundant each one is. Chlorine exists as two isotopes: chlorine-35 (about 75% of all chlorine atoms) and chlorine-37 (about 25%). The weighted average of those two is 35.5. AQA can ask you to calculate Ar from isotope data — the formula is: Ar = Σ(isotope mass × % abundance) ÷ 100.
writeBullets: Relative atomic mass (Ar) = weighted average mass of all isotopes of an element
Weighted average accounts for the abundance of each isotope
Ar = Σ(mass × % abundance) ÷ 100
Example: chlorine Ar = (35 × 75 + 37 × 25) ÷ 100 = 35.5

keyTerms.def: The weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element, compared to 1/12 the mass of carbon-12

keyPoints.memTip: Ar is NOT a whole number because it's an average of isotopes weighted by how common they are. The calculation is always: (mass × abundance) + (mass × abundance), then divide by 100. Practise the chlorine example until you can do the numbers in your head — examiners reuse it.

keyPoints.heading: The history of the atomic model
keyPoints.content: Our picture of the atom has changed dramatically as new experimental evidence came in. Thomson (1897) discovered the electron and proposed the plum pudding model — a sphere of positive charge with electrons embedded in it like fruit in a pudding. Then Rutherford (1911) fired alpha particles at gold foil; most passed straight through, but a few bounced back. This could only mean the atom was mostly empty space with a tiny, dense, positive nucleus — the nuclear model. Bohr (1913) refined it by showing that electrons must orbit the nucleus in fixed shells (energy levels) — each shell has a specific energy. The modern quantum model replaced fixed orbits with probability clouds, but for GCSE you only need Bohr's shells.
writeBullets: Thomson: plum pudding model — electrons embedded in a positive sphere
Rutherford: alpha particle scattering → nuclear model (tiny dense positive nucleus, mostly empty space)
Bohr: electrons orbit in fixed shells/energy levels
Modern quantum model: electron probability clouds (GCSE only needs Bohr's shells)

keyTerms.def: Thomson's model — a positive sphere with electrons embedded in it
Rutherford's model — tiny positive nucleus at the centre, mostly empty space
Rutherford's experiment: alpha particles fired at gold foil; most passed through, some bounced back, revealing a dense nucleus

keyPoints.memTip: TRB — Thomson, Rutherford, Bohr. Each one had new evidence that broke the previous model. Tell the story aloud: 'Thomson thought it was a pudding... then Rutherford fired particles at gold foil and found a nucleus... then Bohr put electrons in shells.' Story beats a list every time.

tables.title: The atomic model — how it changed
rows: Alpha particle scattering — most particles passed through; some bounced straight back
Mathematical work showed electrons must be in fixed energy levels
Further mathematical and spectroscopic evidence (GCSE: just know Bohr's shells)

2.question: An atom has an atomic number of 17 and a mass number of 35. How many neutrons does it have?
2.correctFeedback: Correct — 35 − 17 = 18 neutrons. Mass number minus atomic number gives you the neutrons every time.
2.wrongFeedback: Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 35 − 17 = 18. The atomic number (17) tells you protons; the mass number (35) is protons + neutrons combined.

3.question: Carbon-12 and carbon-14 are isotopes. What is the same in both atoms, and what is different?
options: Same number of neutrons, different number of protons
Same number of protons, different number of neutrons
Same mass number, different atomic number
Same number of electrons, different number of electrons
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Same atomic number (6 protons) — it's still carbon. Different mass number because the number of neutrons differs (6 in carbon-12, 8 in carbon-14).
3.wrongFeedback: Isotopes always have the SAME number of protons (same atomic number — that's what makes them the same element). What changes is the number of NEUTRONS, giving a different mass number.

commonMistakes: The number of neutrons is mass number MINUS atomic number — not mass number minus electrons, not the atomic number itself. Neutrons = mass number − atomic number.
Isotopes have the same number of protons and electrons, but different numbers of neutrons. Their chemical properties are identical because chemistry depends on electron arrangement.

Relative atomic mass is a weighted average — it accounts for how abundant each isotope is, which is why it's not a whole number. Chlorine's Ar of 35.5 comes from ~75% chlorine-35 and ~25% chlorine-37.
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. Electrons only become unequal to protons when the atom forms an ion.

Rutherford's experiment used alpha particles fired at gold foil — not X-rays, not electrons. Most passed through (atom is mostly empty space); a few bounced back (there is a small dense positive nucleus).
examTips: When asked to calculate neutrons, always show the subtraction: neutrons = mass number − atomic number. Don't just write the answer — show the working.
For the atomic model history question (often 6 marks): name each scientist, name their model, and state the evidence that led to the change. Three things for each scientist.
Isotopes questions: confirm same proton number (same element), state the different neutron number, and note that chemical properties are the same (same electron arrangement).
revisionCardBullets: Electron: mass ≈ 0, charge −1, in shells around nucleus
Atomic number = number of protons (= electrons in a neutral atom)
Isotopes: same element, same atomic number, different mass number (different number of neutrons)
Isotopes have identical chemical properties (same electron arrangement)
Relative atomic mass (Ar) = weighted average of all isotopes, accounting for their abundance
Thomson → plum pudding; Rutherford → nuclear model (alpha scattering); Bohr → electrons in shells
Rutherford's alpha particle scattering: most particles passed straight through → atom is mostly empty space; some bounced back → small dense positive nucleus exists
